Francine Prose reviews two new novels—Lee Clay Johnson’s Bloodline and Carl Hiaasen’s Fever Beach—under the banner “Blood in the Game.” Both books, she argues, find ways to register the grotesque violence and corruption of contemporary American life without surrendering the pleasures of fiction.

triadic_ontology.findings: Two new monographs advance the triadic-ontology program. First, Trinitarian Ontologies: Towards a Trinitarian Transformation of Philosophy (Karl Alber, 2025), edited by Eduard Fiedler and Pavel Frývaldský, inaugurates the publisher’s “Studies on Triadic Ontology and Trinitarian Philosophy” series with 425 pages of essays from the 2021 Münster and 2023 Prague conferences. The volume positions triadic ontology as a bridge between analytic and continental traditions, tracing its roots from late-antique theological transformation to contemporary anthropological and political contexts. Contributors including John Milbank and Ryan Haecker explore how trinitarian categories can reconfigure metaphysics beyond the substance–relation impasse. Second, Damiano Migliorini’s Relational Ontologies and Trinitarian Metaphysics: Subsistences, Events and Gunk (academia.edu, 2025) offers a rigorous analytic treatment that integrates quantum-theoretic relational holism with patristic perichoresis. Migliori [TRUNCATED]

ai_systems.findings: Three major advances are converging to create the first truly autonomous AI research systems. Google Cloud's MARS framework (arXiv:2602.02660) introduces budget-aware Monte Carlo planning for AI research, using cost-constrained tree search to balance performance against compute expense—critical when model training can cost thousands per iteration. The system shows 63% of insights come from cross-branch transfer, demonstrating genuine research intuition. InternAgent-1.5 from Shanghai AI Lab (arXiv:2602.08990) takes a different approach, coordinating three specialized subsystems (generation, verification, evolution) to run continuous discovery cycles across both computational and wet-lab domains. Meanwhile, the Mimosa framework (arXiv:2603.28986) focuses on evolution rather than static workflows, automatically synthesizing and refining multi-agent research topologies through experimental feedback. On ScienceAgentBench, Mimosa achieves 43.1% success rates by dynamically discovering optima [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: President Trump intensified his push for UFO disclosure, announcing the Pentagon will release "very interesting" files "very soon" after his February directive to declassify records on extraterrestrial life and UAPs. The All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO) confirmed it's working with the White House to expedite release of "never-before-seen" UAP information, though former AARO director Sean Kirkpatrick cautions expectations, stating no bombshell revelations exist. Congressional momentum continues as Rep. Anna Paulina Luna, chairing the House Oversight Task Force on Declassification, promised a press conference revealing materials of "nonhuman origin" while criticizing Pentagon delays in releasing 46 classified UAP recordings. The Pentagon maintains no verified evidence of extraterrestrial technology exists among hundreds of investigated cases, attributing most sightings to sensor limitations or classified programs.

weather: Today in Edmonds: Sunny and warm, high 75-78°F. Clear skies with only a 5% chance of rain. Light north-northwest winds at 5-10 mph. Evening will be mostly clear, cooling to the mid-50s. Perfect weather for the Edmonds summer market kickoff.

local_news.findings: Construction on the 88th Avenue West Overlay Project will resume the week of May 4, with paving scheduled for May 11-13. The project includes pedestrian improvements and will grind and overlay 88th Avenue West from 194th Street Southwest to 212th Street Southwest. Drivers should expect delays and potential detours during construction hours.

The City of Edmonds will begin its 2025 Combined Cured-in-Place Pipe (CIPP) Rehabilitation Project on May 4 to repair aging sewer and storm pipes throughout the city. The project will use trenchless technology to minimize disruption and is expected to continue through the summer months.

Visible Coffee Roasters will open soon at 622 Fifth Avenue South in the former Barkada location, expanding the local coffee scene in downtown Edmonds. The coffee shop already has established locations in Mill Creek and is known for its specialty roasts.

The Edmonds City Council unanimously adopted the city's first comprehensive Safety Action Plan, which identifies 11 priority traffic safety projects eligible for federal grant funding. The plan aims to eliminate traffic fatalities and serious injuries by 2040, with an interim goal of 50% reduction by 2035.

South County Fire's Board of Commissioners will hold a public hearing on May 5 to discuss a resolution to place a $420 million fire and EMS construction bond before voters in the November general election. The bond would fund rebuilding and renovating fire stations throughout the service area.

family.choir: Seattle Pro Musica has wrapped its 2025-26 auditions, but the door is already open for next year: email auditions@seattlepromusica.org to join the August 2026 mailing list for all-voice-part auditions. Expect an evening slot at Epiphany Parish Seattle (1805 38th Ave, 98122). Requirements: two-minute solo, sight-read two short choral excerpts in quartet, and repeat a phrase by ear. Rehearsals are Tuesday 6:30-9:30 pm; dues $75/yr with scholarships. Seattle Men’s & Women’s Choruses are also recruiting—sign-up form at seattlechoruses.org/join.

Edmonds Library: Toddler Storytime with Cami, Wed May 7 & 14 at 10 am, plus Baby Storytime Fri May 8 & 15 at 10 am. All sessions mix stories, songs, and movement followed by play or art. No registration needed—just drop in.

global_news.findings: The US has escalated its economic pressure campaign against Iran by threatening shipping companies with sanctions if they pay Iranian tolls for passage through the Strait of Hormuz. The US Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) issued an alert warning that payments to Iranian entities could involve "digital assets, offsets, informal swaps, or other in-kind payments." This comes as Iran claims to have collected its first toll revenues from ships navigating the strait, though the BBC could not independently verify this claim. The strait remains effectively closed with only a handful of vessels  [TRUNCATED]

Germany's defense minister Boris Pistorius called the US decision to withdraw 5,000 troops from Germany "foreseeable" as NATO seeks clarification from Washington. The move, which would reduce the US military presence from over 36,000 troops, comes after President Trump criticized German Chancellor Friedrich Merz for saying the US had been "humiliated" by Iranian negotiators. Two senior Republican lawmakers expressed concern about the withdrawal, arguing it sends the wrong signal to Russia. Polish Prime Minister Donald Tusk warned that "the greatest threat to the transatlantic community are not [TRUNCATED]

Prince Harry said he was "devastated" after losing his legal fight with the UK government over police protection, telling the BBC he would "struggle to forgive" the decision and could not safely bring his family to Britain. The Court of Appeal upheld the decision to deny him automatic security protection, with judges saying that while Harry understandably felt aggrieved, this did not amount to an error of law. Harry claimed "security was used as leverage" to try to keep him within the royal fold, and expressed fears about his safety given threats against him and his family.

The European Union has approved a €90 billion loan for Ukraine as the Druzhba oil pipeline was reopened, ending months of deadlock over stalled oil supplies to Hungary. Ukraine announced it had reopened the pipeline after months of stalemate, marking a significant development in European energy security amid ongoing tensions with Russia. The financial package represents one of the largest EU commitments to Ukraine since the war began.

China's economy is showing signs of strain from the ongoing Iran war, with factory orders, costs, and jobs in China's export-driven economy coming under pressure. Despite weathering Trump's tariffs, the Middle East conflict is taking a toll on Chinese manufacturers as global trade disruptions affect supply chains and demand. This represents a significant shift in China's economic outlook as it navigates both US trade tensions and regional conflicts.

intellectual_journals.findings: In a deeply reported long-form investigation, ProPublica and FRONTLINE trace the collapse of the Trump administration’s mass-arrest strategy against immigration protesters.  Over the past ten months federal agents have swept up more than 300 U.S. citizens—bystanders, observers, even family members—during immigration raids, branding them domestic terrorists.  Yet more than one-third of the cases have already crumbled: prosecutors have quietly dismissed charges, refused to file, or lost at trial after video evidence contradicted sworn officer statements.  The pattern, documented through court fi [TRUNCATED]

Jacobin’s spring lead essay, ‘A Post-Order World,’ argues that the post-1945 U.S.-led international system has already ended—not with a bang but with a steady withdrawal of global consent.  Surveying the Trump-ordered abduction of Venezuela’s Maduro, the Greenland troop standoff, and the ongoing Iran bombardment, the piece reframes these events as symptoms of a deeper hegemonic crisis: the material base of American power (industrial supremacy, dollar seigniorage, military reach) has eroded, and with it the ‘spontaneous consent’ that once glued allies to Washington’s agenda.  Rather than a temp [TRUNCATED]

The May 2026 issue devotes its cover package to ‘The Hidden Cause of Heart Disease,’ synthesizing a decade of clinical trials that recast atherosclerosis as an autoimmune phenomenon.  Melinda Wenner Moyer reports that chronic low-grade inflammation—measured by a simple blood marker, hs-CRP—predicts heart attacks more accurately than LDL cholesterol in large cohort studies.  The feature walks through new biologic drugs that quiet interleukin-1β signaling, cutting event rates by 30 percent in high-risk patients without lowering lipid levels.  A companion article explores how lifestyle interventi [TRUNCATED]

The 2026 LRB Winter Lecture series, now in its fifteenth year, will open on 30 January with Oxford professor Seamus Perry on ‘Pluralism and the Modern Poet.’  Perry will test W.H. Auden’s famous worry that a poem embodying liberal-democratic pluralism would be ‘formless, windy, banal and utterly boring,’ revisiting canonical modernists—Auden, MacNeice, Empson—through the lens of contemporary debates about diversity and poetic unity.  The lecture continues the series’ tradition of turning LRB essays into public events; previous installments (Judith Butler on Kafka, Hilary Mantel on royal bodies [TRUNCATED]

Subscription ($9.99/mo) required for full features; results praised as fun but inconsistent. Nirva AI jewelry line focuses on discreet form factors for similar lifelogging.

newyorker.title: Patrick Ball’s Path to Broadway and “Becky Shaw”
newyorker.dek: Before “The Pitt,” the actor waited tables, made lattes, and schlepped Carrie Bradshaw’s wardrobe around town, Rachel Syme writes.
newyorker.text:
Two years ago, the actor Patrick Ball was thirty-four years old and working three jobs in the city, none of them glamorous. He was a server at the Fort Greene restaurant Evelina, a barista at the Chelsea café the Sleeping Cat, and a wardrobe production assistant for the HBO series “And Just Like That . . .” (“driving the costume shoppers around to pick up Carrie Bradshaw’s outfits,” as he described it). His acting career wasn’t going as he had hoped. He’d earned his M.F.A. from Yale’s drama school, in 2022, and had done regional theatre, but he’d had no luck with Broadway. His IMDb page listed just two credits: an episode of “Law & Order” and a straight-to-streaming sci-fi flick called “ReEntry” (as “Bartender”). When he told co-workers that he was still hoping to hit the big time, he recalled, “they’d say, ‘Uh, sure. O.K., dude.’ Then they’d ask me to make an almond-milk latte.”
In 2024, Ball submitted a self-tape for an upcoming HBO Max series called “The Pitt,” which would follow the lives of several E.R. doctors at a crowded teaching hospital in Pittsburgh. It was a good fit, if a long shot; Ball grew up near Greensboro, North Carolina, with an emergency-room-nurse mother and a paramedic father. While waiting to hear back, he started filling out applications for sensible day jobs in nonprofit fund-raising.
It turned out that “The Pitt” was looking for untested talent, and Ball was cast as Dr. Frank Langdon, a resident who struggles with addiction and gets busted for stealing drugs. When Ball told his boss on “And Just Like That . . .” that he was quitting his menial job on one HBO show to take a leading role in a different HBO show, “they were, like, ‘Excuse me, what?’ ” he said. “ ‘Aren’t you the guy who drives us around?’ ”
With “The Pitt” a smash success, recently renewed for a third season, its cast members—many of them, like Ball, previous unknowns—are suddenly in demand. Ball is finally on Broadway, starring in “Becky Shaw,” a dark comedy about a blind date gone wrong. He plays a character he describes as “a toxically nice guy.”
These days, Ball can’t walk down the street without people noticing his cut-glass jawline and chin dimple, and he has taken to wearing a medical mask to hide. On a recent morning, he strapped on the mask and strolled into an office tower near Penn Station; he took the elevator up to Pearl Studios, a warren of rehearsal spaces and audition rooms where he logged a lot of hours during the lean years. “I was such a frequent flier in this building,” he said. “I know exactly how it feels to sit in the hallway with four other dudes who look exactly like you, all wearing the same blue denim shirt, going up for the same part.”
Ball was there to visit the casting director Pat McCorkle, who gave him his first Equity gig, in the play “Shining City” at Barrington Stage Company, in the Berkshires. He ambled through the halls, which smelled of burnt coffee and sweaty socks, until he found a small room where McCorkle sat behind a folding table in a long-sleeved black shirt, ashy blond hair skimming her shoulders. She gave Ball a hug, then exclaimed, “He returns!”
That day, McCorkle and her associates were running an open call for Barrington Stage’s summer season. “I’ve done the high-profile Hollywood thing,” she said. “I helped cast young Matt Damon and Brendan Fraser in ‘School Ties.’ But I far prefer this. I don’t like the work of just hiring Tom Cruise.”
“You were my lifeline,” Ball told her. “You brought me in for everything, even things that I wasn’t even remotely right for.”
“Well, if I like you, I’m going to keep seeing you,” McCorkle said.
“I got to do ‘All My Sons’ in St. Louis and ‘Cat on a Hot Tin Roof’ back in Greensboro,” Ball said. “Here, my agents just kept putting me up for these CW shows about vampires. They just wanted me to be a generic ‘hot guy.’ ”
“Oh, honey,” McCorkle said, with a laugh. “I’ve cast four Hallmark movies_—_I know. I just said, ‘Your time will come.’ ”
Ball rolled up a sleeve to show off some beaded friendship bracelets adorned with phrases like “Da Pitt” and “Ball Is Life.” They were gifts from fans, bestowed at the stage door of the Hayes Theatre, where “Becky Shaw” is playing, as an homage to similar bracelets that Dr. Langdon wears on “The Pitt.” The sudden rush of attention has been overwhelming. Last fall, Ball asked his “Pitt” co-star Noah Wyle, who broke out at twenty-three, on “ER,” for advice on how to handle it. “And Noah said, ‘You’re so lucky this is happening in your thirties,’ ” he recounted. “ ‘You’ve had the opportunity to figure out how to be a regular person in the world.’ ”
Ball mentioned that this chat had happened “on the P.J., flying out from the Emmys.” “P.J.” means “private jet”; Ball is, at least, adapting quickly to the lingo. ♦

literary_pick.title: Gilead
literary_pick.author: Marilynne Robinson
literary_pick.summary: A luminous meditation on faith, family, and mortality told through the voice of Reverend John Ames, a 76-year-old Iowa pastor facing his own death. Written as a letter to his young son, the novel spans three generations of fathers and sons while exploring themes of grace, forgiveness, and the sacred in everyday life. Robinson's prose has been hailed as 'every sentence a work of beauty' and the book has been recognized by the New York Times as one of the 100 best books of the 21st century, cementing its status as a contemporary classic of American literature.

# Trump says Pentagon will release new, 'very interesting' UFO files 'very soon'

WASHINGTON (AP) — President Donald Trump says the Pentagon is preparing to release some "very interesting" UFO files uncovered by his administration, generating a mix of buzz and skepticism as he hints at new revelations around questions of alien life.

Trump started stoking interest in the extraterrestrial in February, directing federal agencies to release their records related to extraterrestrial life and UFOs. S
